epigraph.text:
Give, and it will be given to you. A good measure, 
pressed down, shaken together and running over.

content.text:
Multiplying fractions is surprisingly simple—we just multiply across. 
But why does this work? Understanding the "why" behind the procedure 
makes the math memorable and meaningful.

content.title: Multiplying Fractions
content.content:
To multiply fractions, multiply the numerators together and multiply the 
denominators together:
\[
\frac{a}{b} \times \frac{c}{d} = \frac{a \times c}{b \times d}
\]

content.text:
When we compute $\frac{2}{3} \times \frac{4}{5}$, we're finding 
"$\frac{2}{3}$ of $\frac{4}{5}$." The word "of" in mathematics 
often signals multiplication.

Consider a square representing 1 whole. Divide it into 5 equal columns 
(fifths) and shade 4 of them—that's $\frac{4}{5}$. Now divide the 
square into 3 equal rows (thirds) and consider only 2 of them—that's 
$\frac{2}{3}$.

The overlap is 8 small rectangles out of 15 total. Thus 
$\frac{2}{3}$ of $\frac{4}{5}$ equals $\frac{8}{15}$.

content.title: Fraction of a fraction
content.solution:
"Of" means multiply:
\[
\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{3}{4} = \frac{1 \times 3}{2 \times 4} = \frac{3}{8}
\]
Half of three-fourths is three-eighths.

content.text:
We can simplify before multiplying by canceling common factors between 
any numerator and any denominator. This makes the arithmetic easier.

content.title: Simplify first
content.solution:
**Method 1: Multiply then simplify**
\[
\frac{3}{8} \times \frac{4}{9} = \frac{12}{72} = \frac{1}{6}
\]

**Method 2: Simplify first (cross-cancel)**
\[
\frac{3}{8} \times \frac{4}{9} = \frac{\cancelto{1}{3}}{8} \times \frac{4}{\cancelto{3}{9}} = \frac{1}{\cancelto{2}{8}} \times \frac{\cancelto{1}{4}}{3} = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{3} = \frac{1}{6}
\]
(We canceled 3 from 3 and 9, then 4 from 4 and 8.)

content.title: More cancellation
content.solution:
Look for common factors:

- 5 and 15 share factor 5

- 8 and 12 share factor 4
\[
\frac{5}{12} \times \frac{8}{15} = \frac{\cancelto{1}{5}}{\cancelto{3}{12}} \times \frac{\cancelto{2}{8}}{\cancelto{3}{15}} = \frac{1}{3} \times \frac{2}{3} = \frac{2}{9}
\]

content.text:
To multiply a fraction by a whole number, write the whole number as a 
fraction with denominator 1.
To multiply mixed numbers, first convert them to improper fractions.

content.title: Multiplying Mixed Numbers
content.content:
1. Convert mixed numbers to improper fractions.

2. Multiply the fractions.

3. Simplify and convert back to a mixed number if needed.

content.title: Multiplying mixed numbers
Application of multiplying mixed numbers
content.problem:
A recipe calls for $1\frac{1}{2}$ cups of flour. If you want to make 
$2\frac{1}{2}$ times the recipe, how much flour do you need?
content.solution:
Multiply $1\frac{1}{2} \times 2\frac{1}{2}$:
\[
\frac{3}{2} \times \frac{5}{2} = \frac{15}{4} = 3\frac{3}{4}
\]
You need $3\frac{3}{4}$ cups of flour.

content.title: Fibonacci and Fractions
content.content:
Leonardo of Pisa, known as Fibonacci (c. 1170–1250), wrote extensively 
about fractions in his *Liber Abaci*. He used Egyptian-style unit 
fractions alongside our modern notation, showing merchants how to solve 
practical problems. His book helped replace Roman numerals with the 
Hindu-Arabic system throughout Europe.

marginNotes.text:
But the seed falling on good soil refers 
to someone who hears the word... producing a crop, a hundred, sixty or 
thirty times what was sown.

The integrity of the upright guides 
them, but the unfaithful are destroyed by their duplicity.

Cast your bread upon the waters, for 
you will find it after many days.

marginNotes.author: Richard Feynman
marginNotes.text: What I cannot create, I do not understand.

marginNotes.author: Emmy Noether
marginNotes.text:
My methods are really methods of working and 
thinking; this is why they have crept in everywhere anonymously.
